text: The surgeon dissects the posterior mesorectal plane using a sucker tip to stabilize the endorector device, which visually resembles a sponge.

The surgeon uses the endorector, a sponge-like device, to retract the small bowel by instilling normal saline into it, which adds weight and pulls the bowel away from the surgical field, improving access to the dissection site.
The robotic retractor is visible on the right side of the screen, while the vessel sealer is positioned on the left side, both instruments actively engaged in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a vestacellular device, similar to a ligature device, to seal small to large vessels and achieve hemostasis during the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon uses an additional retractor to facilitate further dissection in the posterior mesorectal plane, enhancing access to the surgical site.
The surgeon performs dissection on the anterior mesorectal plane, retracting the bladder anteriorly to improve access to the retroperitoneal rectum.

The assistant uses a suction device to remove excess blood and fluid from the pelvic area, while also providing additional retraction with a laparoscopic retractor to aid the surgeon's view and access.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler device to divide the rectum. Due to the shorter length of the robotic stapler, additional securement of the mesorectum is achieved using a hemolog device.
The surgeon inspects the proximal resection margin, which appears clear, and washes out the pelvis. The distal resection margin is also visually confirmed to be secure.

The surgeon performs further dissection along the left side of the pelvic brim. The small bowel is positioned below the endorector for optimal retraction, while a laparoscopic retractor is used to retract the sigmoid colon medially.

The surgeon dissects into the diverticular abscess, navigating through inflamed and infected tissue where the anatomical planes are obliterated, making this part of the surgery particularly challenging.

The assistant provides counter traction using a laparoscopic retractor, aiding the surgeon in determining the plane of dissection. The surgeon manipulates two instruments simultaneously within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses three robotic arms to provide retraction on the bowel, moving it away from the surgical field. The assistant provides additional retraction and suctions fluid to aid in determining the dissection plane. The left ureter is visible, a critical structure that must be identified to prevent injury during the procedure.
The surgeon continues dissection deeper into the abscess cavity, carefully maneuvering to avoid breaching the sigmoid colon or encountering major vascular structures.
The surgeon utilizes robotic surgery to achieve enhanced optics and view, entering the abscess cavity. A suction device is used to remove pus, preventing contamination of the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon carefully dissects the abscess cavity, simultaneously retracting and dissecting while the assistant suctions fluid away from the surgical field. This coordinated effort helps in optimally identifying the structures within the dissection line.
The surgeon has moved to the mesocolon and is skeletonizing the inferior mesenteric artery using a vesicella. The artery is being carefully divided as part of the procedure.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis along the mesocolon inferiorly, extending around to the pelvic brim on the left side. The surgical field is challenging due to inflammation and bleeding, complicating the determination of the dissection line.

The surgeon frees the diseased sigmoid colon, revealing its condition. An anal dilator is used to check for strictures in the rectum, followed by resection of an additional distal segment of the rectum.

The surgeon clears the mesorectum and surrounding fat at the stable margin using a laparoscopic 60mm stapler. The entire rectum is resected with one stapler. A circular stapler is introduced into the rectostump, with the surgeon retracting the rectostump to allow the spike of the circular stapler to emerge.

The surgeon performs a staple anastomosis after previously resecting the sigmoid colon and attaching the anvil of the circular stapler. The robotic intracorporeal suturing of the anastomosis suture line is visible.

The surgeon uses laparoscopic scissors to cut an additional suture, completing the anastomosis. The procedure involves injecting endocyanin green intravenously to visualize the vascular supply of the anastomosis using fluorescence.
